steps.voice: The RDM-7 dashboard is built from tiles — RPM, speed, panels, bars, warnings, indicators. Each one is bound to a CAN signal. Let me walk through what you're looking at.
The thick bar across the top is the RPM bar. Yellow fill grows with engine speed. When you pass the redline, it flashes red to warn you.
Turn indicators — left and right arrows beside the RPM number. They mirror your blinker CAN signal so you always know if a blinker's on, even without looking at the cluster.
The big RPM number sits under the bar. Easier to read at a glance than watching the bar fill.
The centre of the screen is the largest-format data — vehicle speed, big and bold, with the current gear directly below.

Eight data panels flank the centre — four on the left, four on the right. Each shows a single signal. The default binding is ignition, MAP, throttle, coolant on the left; intake temp, lambda, oil temp, fuel trim on the right.
You can rebind any panel to any signal — we'll cover that in the Widget Config tutorial. Tap the panel on the dash, pick a new signal, save. Thirty seconds.
Two horizontal bars at the bottom show gradual values as percentages — by default, coolant on the left (blue-to-red gradient) and throttle on the right.
Across the very top are eight warning slots — small circles that light up when a threshold is crossed. You configure threshold, colour, and label in the widget config. Silent until something matters.
Every widget responds in real time — under ten milliseconds from CAN frame to pixel. That's why you can run this as your primary gauge cluster in a track car.
Want to customise any of this? Tap a widget — that opens the config modal. We'll cover that next.
